explanation: We want to specify: Whenever ('has to forward') the Local Node forwards a HELLO message to the MPR Selector Set component, the MPR Selector Set must eventually perform an update. Also, this should hold 'for every DM_i' and 'holds globally' (i.e., always, for all instances and at all times). NATURAL LANGUAGE MAPPING: 'HELLO forwarded to MPR Selector Set' = msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set, 'MPR Selector Set performs update' = update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set. 'forward the HELLO message ALSO' signals a response: whenever the HELLO is forwarded, eventually the update must follow. 'for every DM_i' is implicit in the atomic proposition names (already parameterized). 'This property holds for every...' is captured by the 'Globally' (G) operator. LTL: G(msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set -> F(update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)).

explanation_dict.natural_language: HELLO message forwarded to MPR Selector Set
MPR Selector Set performs an update
for every (globally)
eventually after
Whenever the message is forwarded, eventually an update occurs

explanation: The natural language says that for every DM_i: whenever the Local Node receives a HELLO message (represented by 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node'), it must forward the message to the MPR Selector Set ('msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set'), and then the MPR Selector Set must perform an update ('update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set'). The phrase 'has to forward... also to the MPR Selector Set...which in turn is required to perform an update' describes a required implication between these events. 'This property holds for every DM_i' means it must always be true: thus we use the 'globally' operator G. The temporal ordering is: after a HELLO message is received, forwarding to MPR Selector Set must happen (possibly immediately or eventually), and after that, the update by the MPR Selector Set must happen as well. This can be captured as: globally, whenever 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' happens, then eventually ('F') 'msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set' happens, and after that, eventually 'update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set' happens. To capture the correct sequence, use the 'until' ('U') and 'finally' ('F') operators. One precise formulation is: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F(update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set))). This ensures that, globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent to the Local Node, it is eventually forwarded to the MPR Selector Set and (eventually) leads to an update by the MPR Selector Set.

an update performed by MPR Selector Set

explanation: The requirement is that whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward this message to the MPR Selector Set, and then the MPR Selector Set is required to perform an update. This property applies globally (for every DM_i, i.e., always) and is a causal property: after a 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', eventually 'msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set' happens, and after that eventually 'update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set' happens. To ensure the update happens only after forwarding, we use nested temporal operators: if a HELLO message is sent, then F(msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F(update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)). The conjunction ensures these events are properly ordered. Placing this within a G() operator captures the 'for every DM_i' (globally) part.

explanation: The property states that (for any DM_i) whenever (always, 'for every') a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node has to forward it to the MPR Selector Set, and this in turn must perform an update. The temporal relationship is: any time the event (hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) happens, eventually the MPR Selector Set receives a forwarding (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set), and, eventually after that, an update is performed (update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set). The most natural LTL encoding is: globally, if a HELLO message is sent, then eventually both the message is forwarded to the MPR Selector Set, and after that, an update is performed by MPR Selector Set. This is captured as follows: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)). This means: Whenever a HELLO msg is sent to the local node, at some future point (possibly right away or later) the message is forwarded to MPR Selector Set, and at some time after (possibly immediately) there is an update performed by the MPR Selector Set. If you want to guarantee ordering (forward first, then update), you encode the update to occur after the forward, e.g., G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)).

explanation: The natural language sentence says that whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the message must also be forwarded to the MPR Selector Set component, which then must perform an update. This property has to hold for every DM_i, indicating it is global for all cases. Thus, whenever (globally) a hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, this should be followed by the forwarding to the MPR Selector Set component (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set), which in turn should be followed (i.e., strictly after) by an update performed by MPR Selector Set (update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set). Expressed in LTL: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)). This means that, globally, every time a HELLO message is received by the Local Node, eventually the message is forwarded to the MPR Selector Set, and after that, eventually, an update is performed by that component.

explanation: The main requirement in the natural language is that whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, it must be forwarded to the MPR Selector Set component, which must then perform an update. This is true for every DM_i, so the property should hold globally (G). The sending of the HELLO message is represented by 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node'. Forwarding to the MPR Selector Set is 'msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set'. The update performed by the MPR Selector Set is 'update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set'. The required causality is: after the HELLO message is sent, eventually the message is forwarded to the MPR Selector Set, and eventually that is followed by an update performed by the MPR Selector Set. Thus, for every occurrence of 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', 'msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set' should eventually happen, and after that, 'update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set' should eventually happen as well. This is best captured by nesting the temporal operators: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)). Alternatively, if we want to ensure the full order, we can formulate: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)), which expresses that after the HELLO message is sent, eventually both (1) message is forwarded to the MPR Selector Set and (2) after that, eventually the update is performed. If the update must not happen before forwarding, the stronger version is: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)).

the message is forwarded to the MPR Selector Set component

property holds globally (for every DM_i)

explanation: The sentence describes that whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node (atomic proposition: hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), the Local Node should forward the message to the MPR Selector Set ('msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set'), which should then perform an update ('update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set'). The property must hold for every DM_i, so we take this as a global property (i.e., globally, whenever hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, eventually the message must be forwarded to the MPR Selector Set, and eventually after that, an update must be performed by the MPR Selector Set). The LTL implication and temporal ordering can be expressed using the temporal operators. Since the forwarding should always be eventually followed by an update, and both must happen after the initial sending, we use: 'G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F(update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set))))'. This says: globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, eventually the message is forwarded to the MPR Selector Set and, possibly in the future after that, eventually an update is performed by the MPR Selector Set. If you wish to ensure that the forwarding always happens before the update, and that the update may only take place after the forwarding, a stricter variant using the 'U' ('until') operator is: 'G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set) & (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set U update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)))'. Both forms are frequently encountered in specification writing; we'll use the more precise 'until' form here.

the message is forwarded to the MPR Selector Set component

explanation: The requirement is that whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward the message to the MPR Selector Set component, and in turn, the MPR Selector Set component must perform an update. This is to be enforced for all DM_i ('for every DM_i'), meaning it should hold globally (G). So, for every occurrence of 'hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', eventually the message is forwarded to MPR Selector Set ('msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set'), and eventually after that, the MPR Selector Set performs an update ('update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set'). This can be represented as: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set) & F(update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set))). Optionally, to strictly enforce ordering (that is, the update occurs after the forwarding), you could further nest the formulas, but the most direct translation is as above.

explanation: The property describes that whenever a HELLO message has been sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward it to the MPR Selector Set component, and, in turn, the MPR Selector Set component is required to perform an update. Since the property says this should happen for every DM_i and doesn't specify any exception, 'always' (G) applies. This can be encoded as: globally, if a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, then eventually (in some future) the message is forwarded to the MPR Selector Set, and (after that) an update is performed by that component. In LTL, this is usually written as: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set & F update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set)). This ensures that whenever the message is sent, both actions eventually happen, in order (the forwarding to the MPR Selector Set may happen before the update, but both must follow the initial sending).

explanation: The property states that whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must eventually forward the message to the MPR Selector Set, which must in turn perform an update. 'This property holds for every DM_i' implies that the formula is globally (G) true. The chain is: when hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, then eventually (F) msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set occurs, and after that, eventually update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set occurs. Thus, the implication is: if hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, then eventually msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set AND after that, eventually update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set. In LTL, it is common to enforce that update only happens after forward, so we use two steps: globally, whenever hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, then it's always the case that eventually msg_forwarded_to_mpr_selector_set occurs, and after that, eventually update_performed_by_mpr_selector_set occurs.
